data.title: The AI Narrative: Something Doesn't Compute
data.description:
There are many chinks developing around the AI Narrative that has driven most of the gains in the stock market over the past three years. The buildout of AI infrastructure has also been a key economic engine as well. If investors start to doubt the AI Revolution and Big Tech slows the pace of investments, both the equity markets and the economy will be in deep trouble. It appears these huge capital expenditures will soon start to reduce stock buybacks as well.

Unfortunately, there are many problems with the AI story. The first challenge is more and more fund managers are starting to question whether an AI bubble has formed. Some skepticism is long overdue, in my opinion. The huge stock market rallies in Big Tech have resulted in the largest 10 companies representing approximately 40% of overall market capitalization. Stocks have not been so bifurcated since late in the Internet Boom.

NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has rallied more than 1,300% over the past half decade and now has a market capitalization north of $4.5 trillion. This is roughly equivalent to the annual GDP of Germany, the largest economy in Europe.

The second thing getting much more scrutiny is the vendor financing circle that is powering so much of the rally in AI-related stocks. This ecosystem is reminiscent of the vendor financing cycle powered by the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO) in the late 1990s, but on steroids.

The graph above has gone quite viral over the past month. It also helps explain how OpenAI, a company bleeding cash, can have some $1.4 trillion in future commitments over the next eight years.

This is despite having revenue of around $14 billion in 2025 and leaving the year with a sales run rate of approximately $20 billion.

There are also growing questions around whether the hundreds of billions being spent annually on AI infrastructure will ever generate an acceptable return on investment. Meta Platforms (META) recently disclosed it is committing over $600 billion in the United States by 2028 with its third-quarter results. This funding will go to support AI technology, infrastructure, and workforce expansion. The disclosure has helped push the stock down some 20% since Q3 numbers hit the wires, despite better-than-expected third-quarter revenue growth of over 26% on a year-over-year basis.

Investors appear to have become more skeptical around these huge investments, especially as some of these tech giants are starting to issue debt to fund these massive bets. Oracle (ORCL) is looking to raise $38 billion in debt sales to help fund its AI buildout. Oracle already has a concerning debt-to-equity ratio, it should be noted.

These growing concerns have helped push ORCL stock down some 30% over the past month.

Finally, even if all these concerns ebb in the weeks and months ahead, there is one challenge with the AI Revolution that will be very difficult to overcome in the years ahead. This could soon undermine the AI Narrative that has been the primary driver of the stock market rally over the past 36 months.

Simply put, where will all the electricity to power these massive data centers emanate from? And unfortunately, renewable sources like solar and wind do not have the reliability to deliver this electricity. This has resulted in some interesting 180-degree turns. Bill Gates, one of several tech billionaires who have advocated for climate change mitigation and the need for alt-energy for many years, has recently largely abandoned that crusade.

Mr. Gates has been a long-term advocate of nuclear power. He is not the only one showing nuclear power new love. Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently announced the first power purchase agreement between a U.S. utility and an advanced nuclear developer this summer. Even the infamous Three Mile Island is being brought online to provide power to data centers. The Trump administration also just struck a deal last month with the owners of Westinghouse. The agreement is to invest $80 billion to build new nuclear plants across the United States. There were 94 nuclear reactors in operation across the United States in 2024.

Unfortunately, new nuclear generation takes years and, in some cases, over a decade to deliver new capacity. Smaller, more modular solutions might eventually help address this constraint. Given the huge reserves of natural gas in the United States, this will likely be the primary way to bring on new electricity generation until the gap can be filled by nuclear.

Meta Platforms recently signed a huge deal with Entergy Corporation (ETR) to build new natural gas plants to power its massive AI data center it is moving forward with in Louisiana. Many more of these deals will likely happen in the quarters and years ahead.

The problem is there could be a huge power generation shortfall based on the projected data center buildout. Morgan Stanley (see above) has projected that the United States could face a power shortage of up to 20% by 2028 due to the data center buildout. This is a shortfall of up to 44 nuclear reactors.

So, even if all the current questions around AI can be resolved around vendor financing, revenues, and valuations, the thing that doesn't compute for me is how the electricity needed for the AI Revolution will be supplied in the years ahead. And with markets trading at extreme valuations, this eventual realization could be what eventually pops the AI Bubble.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Stock: Buy And Forget
data.description:
Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) shareholders have had a 2025, to forget as the stock has tumbled due to increased competition, pressure from the U.S. government to lower drug prices, and a pared-back financial outlook. While the current situation looks bleak, I think NVO is a solid long-term pick for investors that are willing to wait a while to see results.

The chart above tells an ugly story, one that entails NVO dropping more than 50% over the last 12 months as investors have fled in the face of company-specific and macro risks, which have all emerged simultaneously. Novo Nordisk has been the market leader in the type 2 diabetes and obesity market for a few years now, as its cash cow drugs Ozempic (for diabetes type 2 indication) and Wegovy (for weight loss indication) have dominated sales and generated outsized profits for the company.

This led to a substantial increase in share price as investors piled in with the promise of growing demand for these drugs and Novo Nordisk's leading position in the space. The company's operating results speak for themselves:

While Ozempic was approved by the FDA in 2017, it didn't really start taking off until 2021, the same year Wegovy was approved. In the 4 years since, net income has just about doubled as these drugs have become ever more popular and generated ever more sales. If you noticed the dip in net income in the top chart, that's because, as is often the case in pharma, a monopoly like this never lasts long.

Eli Lilly (LLY) entered the fray with Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes in 2022 and Zepbound for weight loss in late 2023, bringing competition and an even more effective treatment to the market. There have been many studies on the subject, but the scientific consensus is that the active ingredient in Eli Lilly's drugs, tirzepatide, leads to superior outcomes relative to Novo's semaglutide. While there has been a bit of a delayed fuse, it is now evident that Eli Lilly's products are taking significant market share and leading to a worse outlook for Novo Nordisk.

Specifically, Ozempic and Wegovy are both seeing rapid share loss to Zepbound and Mounjaro and have, in all likelihood, passed their peak profitability:

While one's initial reaction to this might be that a 50%+ decline in share price in response to a simple plateauing of sales for these two drugs seems a bit overdone, I should note that Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus (an oral version of semaglutide) combined generated more than 70% of Novo Nordisk's total sales in H1 2025. In contrast to NVO's downward spiral, LLY has appreciated by 20% over the same timeframe, indicating investors are betting on a market positioning swap in the quarters and years ahead. And this is just one headwind.

The second factor keeping NVO down is the U.S. government's push to reduce drug prices in the face of rising healthcare costs. I won't dwell on this one too long because I don't think it's as important to the stock's prospects, but the primary fears have been that an imposed reduction in Ozempic and Wegovy prices will hurt the company's bottom line at a time when the market has become laser focused on the profitability of these products.

And in fact, just last week, Novo Nordisk announced that it had agreed, along with Eli Lilly, to a deal with the Trump administration to reduce the cost of its drugs to Medicare, Medicaid, and other customers. While good for the consumer, this appears, on the surface, to be a negative catalyst for the company and its peers -- lower prices means lower sales and lower profit margins. The reason I'm not as concerned about this risk is because the cost decrease will likely make these drugs more accessible, increasing units sold and at least partially offsetting some of the price pressure. How much is impossible to say, but I think investors should focus on competitive pressures when weighing an investment in NVO.

On that front, the near-term is looking rather grim. Eli Lilly has a superior product, and, while Ozempic has a powerful brand name, that only buys you so much goodwill. To bolster its competitive position, Novo Nordisk attempted to vulture weight loss biotech Metsera (MTSR) after Pfizer (PFE) made an initial bid in late September. After some back and forth, lawsuits, and a call from the FTC, Metsera announced that it would be accepting Pfizer's offer. Working against Novo Nordisk was its already established position in the weight loss market, which prompted the FTC to express doubt that a potential acquisition would clear anti-trust hurdles.

Of interest is Metsera's injectable GLP-1 agonist MET-097i, which is currently going through clinical trials and has the potential for once-monthly dosing, and a broader pipeline of other injectable and oral GLP-1 candidates. It's apparent why Novo Nordisk was interested in such a pipeline with its current offerings showing signs of weakness, and it's pretty ironic that it's dominant market position seems to have prevented an acquisition that might shore up its rapidly weakening moat.

I think this illustrates well why NVO has fallen so precipitously -- Eli Lilly is surging with a better drug, Pfizer just spent (up to) $10 billion to join the fray with promising candidates, and suddenly the lucrative weight loss market is looking a bit crowded.

Okay, so that all sounds pretty bad, right? And in the short-term, all of these factors are likely to weigh on NVO, and I'd be surprised if the stock is able to generate much positive momentum. However, for those with a long-term outlook that are willing to wait potentially years to see the fruits of holding the stock, I think NVO presents an asymmetric risk-reward profile.

Even at reduced guidance, Novo Nordisk expects operating profit to grow between 4% and 7% for FY2025, which is much lower than previous projections, but I think represents a bottoming out of operating results. Even with increased competition, Wegovy and Ozempic will have a highly profitable long tail that will continue to generate free cash flow and provide funds for Novo Nordisk to spend on R&D in order to develop its already vast obesity and type 2 diabetes pipeline as well as to expand into new markets.

Even in what is shaping up to be a major down year, the company still earned nearly $10 billion in FCF for the first 9 months of the 2025 fiscal year, more than covering its ordinary and interim dividend payouts and shoring up the attractive 3.44% dividend yield. Despite a $14 billion debt pile, I think the balance sheet is in fine shape on the back of the aforementioned strong cash flows and $5.1 billion in cash & equivalents.

And while market share is currently in flux in the U.S., international penetration remains a growth avenue:

While share is dropping internationally in response to the previous competitive pressures mentioned, Novo Nordisk still enjoyed a whopping 68% volume market share as of August 2025 -- even in its decline, these drugs will generate massive cash flows for the company.

More broadly, this is the pharma cycle: first to market takes the share and generates outsized profits, competitors and generics enter and drive margins down, the original drug generates a long tail of pure profit, a new drug enters the market and begins the cycle anew. Novo Nordisk is undoubtedly in the trough of this cycle, and operating results will be impacted. However, this has certainly not surprised management, and the company has a robust pipeline of drugs to regain market share.

On the valuation side, I like to use historical P/E when a stock has dropped so much since it can be a good indicator of where the bottom is (assuming fundamentals remain intact, which, for NVO, I believe they have):

There are obviously some extenuating circumstances that could be argued to make this drop different (major company restructuring, competition with its cash cow drugs, government pricing pressure), but this sell-off appears overdone, in my opinion. While I don't expect sentiment to turn too positive anytime soon, I think we are near the bottom, if we haven't passed it already.

Investor Takeaway

The bottom line here is that NVO looks like a solid long-term play for those with the patience to ride out some dead air and further downside potential. Novo Nordisk has strong cash flow, an attractive yield, cash cow drugs that will be earnings profit for many years despite some pressure, and a robust product pipeline that will keep the company competing in the market for a long time to come.

I would caution those that are looking to catch this falling knife for a quick buck on a turnaround that NVO does not currently appear primed for such a move. Market share losses will likely continue, and it may be a couple of years before the company sees a return to the growth numbers that brought it up to all-time highs. But for those with the conviction to hold the stock in their portfolios for a while, and reap the dividend while they wait, I think this is a bet worth taking.

As such, I am rating NVO a Buy.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why I Have Been (And Remain) Negative On Consumer Stocks
data.description:
Intro

This is an atypical article from me. Instead of what I usually write, stock analyses, this time I am presenting a bird's-eye view article with some thoughts about why I think that consumer stocks a) deserve to be losing and b) the malaise is unlikely to end anytime soon.

Why consumer stocks will likely continue to have a hard time

I must admit I am surprised how offended some people can become when their widows and orphans stocks, mainly consumer staples with reliable dividends, are attacked (see e.g., my articles about General Mills, Diageo, Conagra Brands, Colgate-Palmolive, Constellation Brands, etc.).

It is no secret that I've been writing and commenting negatively about them for some time. And I was right in most cases, as these "safe bets," which for many investors belong in every defensive portfolio, have performed very poorly. I stick to my view that the dividends won't be safe over time. Here's why I think so.

One of the points I am making is to remind my readers not to live in the past.

While nostalgia is a great thing in certain areas (cars, homes, precious collectibles, holidays, or any other positive memories, etc.), in investing it is suicide. Sooner or later people discover this. Unfortunately, often in a painful way. Whether one is affected directly or just through the observation of others, there is a time when certain investment topics work out. And there is a time when themes and topics break. Some are of a cyclical nature; others are deeply rooted and structural.

I view the consumer staples challenges as structural.

Most consumer stocks are having a hard time because reality is hitting hard. It has nothing to do with investors preferring hot Mag 7 or AI stocks, growth over value, or any other reason you are hearing and reading every day. It is a structural break. A huge market force that, for some time, could have been ignored. And I am absolutely sure you all are absolutely aware of the following.

The chart starts in 1950 with a figure of 37, meaning 37 newborns per 1,000 people – or 3.7 per 100. At that pace, each population nearly doubled. But it was short-lived. Everyone knows the trend has been down.

People also know, whether consciously or not, that a figure of less than 2.1 means the population is not growing anymore (2 = stable). 17.13 sounds like a great figure, but it is per 1,000, not per 100 – it's 1.7 children per woman (respectively family).

1.7 means the pool of potential and future consumers is shrinking fast.

This is the key sentence and message of this article. And this is for the entire world population, already including highly productive and fertile countries and regions. The majority does not seem to care. And to ignore it. The next chart shows a detailed overview.

We can see two things.

First, for ALL regions, the trend is down, including Africa, the region with the highest birth rates. And second, even though the world average in this case looks to be slightly above the critical threshold of 2.0, this figure is boosted by the black line representing Africa. The rest of the world is below 2.0, i.e., in shrinking mode. The two biggest losers, so to speak, are Europe in red and North America in yellow, where the figure in some countries is even closer to 1 than 2. See, for example, a piece of news I stumbled upon recently (see here).

Most countries are simply running below their replacement rate, effectively shrinking demand and the supply of consumers.

But why have many consumer stocks only recently (a few years, max a decade) started to experience painful and prolonged bear markets?

For some time, the companies were able to balance this highly negative business factor through offshoring, importing cheaper goods, automation, price hikes, tricks like shrinkflation or quality downgrades, mergers and market consolidations, and other measures. Also, economies were growing faster, countries had less debt, and people simply had more left over to spend and consume. But it becomes harder and harder to keep the wheel spinning.

At a certain point, goods become too expensive, forcing people to trade down, like in the current environment where perceived brand values turned out to evaporate (something I have been criticizing in some of my previous articles) – until recently absolutely unthinkable for many. Pricing power – seems to be a relict of the past. Private label brands stand ready. Mass layoffs and a weak consumer sentiment come on top, not necessarily a tailwind, exercising pressure from a different direction.

And third, something I have been pointing to in my analyses, are the high debt piles these consumer companies amassed in assumption of eternal growth and stable business models. Bad luck, as not only did the end of zero interest rates catch many companies on the wrong foot. But plenty of debt facing a structurally challenged business environment has created a sclerosis these retail darlings can hardly escape from.

One solution would be to massively downsize, to aggressively pay down debt, and to write off brand values that are based on decades-old memories and less so contemporary reality.

And in many cases, dividend cuts.

No one wants to hear this, though. Of course, investors do not, but management does not either. No one wants to touch thought-to-be-safe dividends. Else, these stocks will find themselves another floor lower. In my view, this explains the underlying mechanics better than "Mr. Market doesn't see the value."

I am staying out of such stocks. I won't be eating twice as much, nor will I, in any other shape or form, contribute to higher consumption beyond what's necessary, respectively possible at all.

And the argument of emerging markets being the growth driver, pulling up the average, has its flaws, too. My guess is that these countries at some point will want to establish their own industries and companies. Not only to better represent and satisfy cultural differences. But to have better control and oversight, as well as to simply favor domestic interests, instead of buying foreign candy bars or cereals.

Conclusion

In short, the tricks ran out of gas. Consumer stocks aren't dying – they'll just need to adapt to a leaner world. But we are not there yet. For investors, it’s a rotation signal: Favor companies that either are not aligned to these industries or target direct beneficiaries like discounters over premium brands.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Reasonably Valued With One Key Caveat
data.description:
Shares of social media behemoth Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) fell 11% last week after a very impressive Q3 2025, as concerns over its announced outsized spend on AI data center infrastructure weighed. With ~$600 billion earmarked for a still-to-be-defined revenue opportunity – harkening a repeat of Metaverse concerns – the largest one-day stock price drop in three years merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Although it really needs no introduction, Meta Platforms, Inc. is a Menlo Park, California based series of social media platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, Threads, and WhatsApp constructs. The company is also investing in experimental technologies in the hopes of creating the next wave of user connectivity, housed in its Reality Labs division. Meta was formed in 2004 as TheFacebook and went public in 2012, raising net proceeds of $6.8 billion at $38 per share. The stock trades around $610.00 a share, equating to a market cap of $1.54 trillion.

The company is capitalized by two classes of stock. The 2.18 billion shares of publicly traded Class A stock confer economic interest and one vote per share. The 342.6 million shares of privately held Class B stock bestow economic interest and ten votes per share, providing Founder, Chairman, and CEO Mark Zuckerberg with control of corporate direction.

Meta in a Nutshell

Meta essentially derives 99% of its top line ($189.5 billion total in the 12 months ending September 30, 2025 (TTM)) from advertising revenue generated by the aforementioned social media platforms, known as Family of Apps (FoA). A staggering 3.54 billion people visited one of these sites daily in September 2025. During the past five years, an increasing amount of the profits from FoA have been earmarked for Reality Labs, which was founded in 2020, initially to develop augmented and virtual reality devices as part of a larger concept to create what it refers to as the Metaverse. To perpetuate this vision, next-generation connectivity, and more effective advertising via AI, the company has (and will continue) to invest substantially in data centers.

The Key Issue

The core of this report will summarize the one key issue pertaining to Meta, which is its enormous investment in AI data centers to engender the Metaverse among many other applications.

Meta Platforms has started to raise concerns from investors around its spellbinding spend on the buildout of its AI data center infrastructure. Projected to outlay $71 billion on capex in FY25, Meta announced in September 2025 that it was committed to spending ~$600 billion in American infrastructure through 2028 – later confirmed on November 7, 2025 – the biggest piece of which is AI data centers. Translated, this pledge suggests ~$400 billion plus of capex spend 2026-2028.

The question becomes, “what return is Meta anticipating from this massive investment?” The question has merit not only due to the magnitude of the investment but also owing to Zuckerberg’s recent track record on considerable technology spends. Exhibit A is the $73 billion of accumulated losses for the buildout of the Metaverse virtual world via Reality Labs through Q3 2025. Exhibit B is Meta’s ~$100 billion spend on virtual and augmented reality since 2014. In the first nine months of FY 2025 (YTD 2025), those ‘investments’ garnered a loss of $13.2 billion on revenue of $1.3 billion, consisting of sales of VR goggles, Meta eyewear, and sundry games and apps. To date, those technology forays have been abysmal failures.

Q3 2025 Financials & Outlook

As such, the biggest drop in the company’s share price in three years occurred on October 30, 2025, when it reported Q3 2025 financials, posting earnings of $7.25 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $51.2 billion versus $6.03 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $40.6 billion, representing improvements of 20% and 26%, respectively. The headline (GAAP) bottom-line figure of $1.05 per share was impacted by a non-cash income tax charge related to the Big Beautiful Bill.

The impressive results easily beat the consensus forecast of $6.67 per share on revenue of $49.4 billion, bolstered by 3.54 billion average daily active people on its platforms in September 2025, representing an 8% year-over-year increase, a 14% year-over-year increase in ad impressions during the quarter, and a 10% rise in the average price per ad.

Zuckerberg touted the early returns from his investment in AI, with time on Facebook up 5% year-over-year and video time spent on Instagram up over 30%. He credited AI with a 5% increase in ad conversions on Instagram and 3% on Facebook. Zuckerberg then provided a twofold justification of his aggressive spend on AI infrastructure. First, he stated that Meta “was are sort of perennially operating the FoA and ads business in a compute-starved state” – in other words, the buildout was required to keep up. In the case of an overbuild, it would provide the company with optionality (e.g., “standing up an API service”) to offer additional functionality and services. Second, the company wanted to be prepared for the most optimistic (soonest) case for superintelligence – a construct where AI surpasses the best human minds across every domain – although the revenue opportunity is largely undefined, except to say that the company was “developing [its] next generation of models and products [that he]…was looking forward to sharing more on that front over the coming months.”

Owing to market skepticism regarding Zuckerberg’s AI data center ambitions, shares of META declined 11% to $666.47 in the subsequent trading session. They have fallen an additional eight percent since.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Although the company generates substantial cash from operations ($79.6 billion YTD 2025), it will likely not be enough to cover the staggering surge in capex spending expected over the next three years. As such, taking advantage of tight corporate spreads, Meta executed a $30 billion debt offering across six maturities out to 2065 on November 3, 2025, raising its debt to $58.8 billion against cash and securities of $44.4 billion (as of September 30, 2025). The company does pay a quarterly dividend of $0.525 per share, for a modest current yield of 0.3%. Meta also returns capital to shareholders in the form of stock buybacks, repurchasing 40 million shares for $26.3 billion in YTD 2025.

Harkening the reincarnation of Metaverse, Zuckerberg’s comments actuated some negativity among sell-side analysts, including a downgrade from Oppenheimer from an outperform to a hold, while price targets were lowered at no fewer than 18 firms. That said, the Street is still extremely bullish on the company’s prospects, featuring 58 buy or outperform ratings against nine holds and one underperform with a mean price objective of just over $840.00 a share. On average, the prognosticators expect Meta to earn $26.11 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $199.32 billion in FY 2025, followed by $30.33 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $234.76 billion in FY 2026.

Verdict

Given the recent drop in price, Meta is not outrageously valued at 20 times FY 2026E EPS, and its debt level is far from worrisome. That said, there are headwinds. First is the fact that nearly half the Earth’s population logs onto one of its platforms every day, suggesting that its volume growth is limited. And even if every person on the planet eventually consumes content from the company’s platforms daily, the perception that volume growth is slowing will act as a drag on its stock.

Most importantly, until the revenue opportunity from its ongoing $600 billion investment in AI data center infrastructure is better defined, the perception that Zuckerberg has made another blunder – whether true or not – will also act as an anchor on Meta’s stock. Its social media platforms are incredible ad revenue machines, but the company’s indulgent AI data center spend could be perceived by the market as an impulsive attempt to extend its extraordinarily impressive run.

The bet here is that multiple compression continues as its free cash flow is pinched over the next three years, keeping me on the sidelines on Meta for now.

data.title: XLB's Tough Year: Weak Momentum, Rising Risks, And No Clear Turnaround
data.description:
The Materials sector has struggled despite apparent tailwinds. For instance, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is down 8% on the year, near 99. What’s more, gold stocks have soared in 2025, which should be bullish for the broader resource space. Nevertheless, the Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) is up just 4% over the past 12 months, underperforming the S&P 500 by about 12 percentage points.

I had a "Hold" rating on the fund back in July, and I reiterate that cautious stance today. The valuation remains decent, but technical patterns are not conducive to gains into year-end, in my view.

S&P 500 Sector ETF Performances

S&P 500 Sector Valuations

According to the issuer, XLB "seeks to provide precise exposure to companies in the chemical, construction material, containers and packaging, metals and mining, and paper and forest products industries." Tracking the S&P 500 Materials sector index, the fund allows investors to take strategic or tactical positions at a more targeted level than traditional style-based investing.

XLB is a large ETF with $5.3 billion in assets under management as of Nov. 13, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is low at just eight basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is 1.96%, which is about 0.7 percentage points higher than the S&P 500’s.

Share-price momentum has been very weak lately, earning the product a poor D- ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, XLB has elevated historical standard deviation trends, while the realized volatility rate is higher than the VIX, for instance.

Still, the ETF scores well on liquidity, thanks to an average daily volume of more than 6.5 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is just two basis points, per SSGA Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. The plurality of assets, 30%, are housed in the upper-left section, indicating significant holdings in U.S. large-cap value.

I’d also call out that more than half of the allocation is classified as mid-cap, which adds to cyclical risks. Materials, of course, is among the most economically sensitive sectors, so if we see reduced U.S. GDP growth prospects, then the fund could underperform further.

The good news is that the price-to-earnings ratio has drifted down to just 16.9x. Couple that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 11.4%, and the resulting PEG ratio is actually attractive at 1.48x.

XLB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector view, the 100% Materials fund is highly concentrated—another risk factor to weigh. In all, the top 10 stocks account for more than 60% of the fund.

Thus, keeping tabs on both the fundamentals and technicals of the largest positions is key, particularly during earnings season (which is wrapping up for Q3). Finally, the dividend payout has been rather steady since 2020.

XLB: Holdings & Dividend Information

In terms of seasonality, November has historically been a bullish month, but the fund is negative this go-round. When prices buck the usual calendar trends, investors should take notice.

Moreover, the December through January period has not been all that strong in the past 10 years.

XLB: Weak Dec.-Jan. Trends

The Technical Take

With a modest valuation and relative underperformance for most of this year, XLB’s technical situation is mixed at best. Notice in the chart below that shares are now below their long-term 200-day moving average. Furthermore, the 200 DMA is on the decline, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it printed a bearish divergence to price back in the third quarter.

Adding to the lukewarm trends are volume considerations. The volume by price profile on the left side of the chart reveals that there’s a large amount of shares traded from $80 to $90. That will make rally attempts tough on the bulls. Additionally, volume rose as XLB fell in October into early November.

In terms of support and resistance, $92-$93 is where sellers may come about, while support is likely between $83 and $84.

XLB: Shares Mired In a Range, Falling 200 DMA

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on XLB. Shares are down 3% since my summer analysis, and I just don’t see enough of a turnaround or washout to raise it to a buy.

data.title: Spire: Data Center Thesis May Be Fraying, But Company Still On Growth Trajectory
data.description:
On Friday, November 14, 2025, natural gas utility Spire Inc. (SR) announced its fourth quarter 2025 (the company uses an October through September fiscal year) earnings results. The initial reaction to this earnings release was sharply negative, as Spire’s shares fell in the premarket trading session:

It is possible that this was due to disappointment with the company’s earnings report, as several of its financial metrics came in weaker than the company’s fourth quarter 2024 numbers:

Financial Metric

With the exception of revenues, all of these figures were rather disappointing. They certainly do not point to a company that is managing to grow its profits over time. The company’s 5.7% dividend hike suggests that management is confident that the losses in this quarter will prove to be a one-off event, as is the guidance range that puts the company’s long-term earnings per share growth at 5% to 7% annually. If the company actually manages to achieve that, then, combined with its new yield of 3.77%, the company should be able to deliver a 9% to 11% annual return if its multiples remain the same as they are today. This is not a bad return to expect from a utility stock, and indeed many of them target a very similar long-term total return.

Spire has managed to deliver a total return in that ballpark over the past five years, despite the weakness in 2023 and, of course, the stock price decline in the pre-market trading session this morning. Over the past five years, the company has delivered a 68.22% total return:

A significant proportion of this came in the past twelve months, though, which we should keep in mind. Spire’s total return year-to-date is 31.87% and its total return over the past twelve months is 38.79%:

Thus, over half of the company’s trailing five-year total return came in the past twelve months. This could explain some of the weakness that we saw in the pre-market session alongside the earnings report.

Potential Holes In The Artificial Intelligence Thesis

Regular readers can likely remember my previous article on Spire, in which I stated that the expansion of cloud computing and generative artificial intelligence could prove to be a growth driver for Spire. From that article:

This seems rather counterintuitive since none of these companies are actually involved in the development of artificial intelligence, and data centers are powered by electricity instead of natural gas. However, it is indeed the electric-hungry nature of artificial intelligence data centers that is beneficial for natural gas utilities.

I also explained in that article that data center developers have begun putting natural gas turbines on-site so that they can generate their own electricity to power their computing equipment rather than wait several years for a connection to the electric grid. This thesis, combined with the fact that Kansas City, Missouri, has been a growing market for data center development in recent years, may have been a contributing factor to the incredibly strong returns that Spire has delivered year-to-date.

However, there are currently some signs that the euphoria around artificial intelligence may be overblown. A few weeks ago, Bloomberg published an article discussing the circular deals involving some of the major players in the generative artificial intelligence sector. JP Morgan’s private bank also realized that this is going on and it could be a major problem for investors who are betting on this technology.

As Michael Cembalest of JP Morgan’s private bank points out:

Oracle’s stock jumped by 25% after being promised $60 billion a year from OpenAI, an amount of money OpenAI doesn’t earn yet, to provide cloud computing facilities that Oracle hasn’t built yet, and which will require 4.5 GW of power (the equivalent of 2.25 Hoover Dams or four nuclear power plants), as well as increased borrowing by Oracle whose debt to equity ratio is already 500% compared to 50% for Amazon, 30% for Microsoft, and even less at Meta or Google.

Mr. Cembalest’s conclusion is that Amazon.com (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG) will have to either massively dilute their existing shareholders or borrow enormous amounts of money in the debt markets in order to defend their turf and maintain their relevance in a world that is moving towards the use of generative artificial intelligence. The alternative, of course, is that generative artificial intelligence will not become nearly as widespread as what investors currently assume will be the case. As I pointed out in a recent article, 95% of companies that attempt to deploy artificial intelligence are not making any money from it. OpenAI (OPENAI) itself is not profitable, and CEO Sam Altman recently suggested in an interview that some sort of government backing may prove to be necessary. In other words, it is very possible that the financial realities of the generative artificial intelligence industry will ultimately prove to prevent it from living up to all of the hype that is currently surrounding it.

The market appears to be waking up to this as well. This morning, ZeroHedge reported that the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and the NASDAQ-100 Index (NDX) sold off in the pre-market futures market as investors began to get worried about the enormous amounts of debt financing that companies in the artificial intelligence space will need to issue in order to live up to the hype, and even then, there is no guarantee that they will be able to make a profit from it. We can see this quite clearly in the fact that the mega-cap members of the so-called “Magnificent 7” (MAGS) sold off more than the rest of the market in the pre-market session.

This all suggests that the pre-market session selloff in Spire may not have been entirely due to disappointment with the earnings report. General investor concern about the viability of the generative artificial intelligence narrative and boom may have driven the company’s stock price down. After all, if the industry ultimately fails to live up to the hype, then it is quite possible that there will not be a substantial number of new data centers constructed within the company’s service territory that require natural gas to power them. This weakens the thesis that I presented in the previous article on this company.

Interest Rate Cuts Less Likely

Another possible contributing factor to the company’s sell-off today comes from expectations about the actions that the Federal Reserve might take in December with respect to interest rates.

Yesterday, Neel Kashkari, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, stated that he is unsure whether or not a cut to the federal funds target rate would be appropriate in December. Notably, Mr. Kashkari was one of the members of the Federal Open Market Committee who did not support the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cut in October, as he believes that the economy remains strong enough to support higher rates and inflation is still too high to risk a resurgence.

His statement yesterday means that there are now five voting members of the FOMC who do not believe that a December interest rate cut is appropriate. There are twelve voting members of the FOMC, so just under half of them have now come out and publicly opposed a December interest rate cut. This has caused the market to become doubtful about whether or not an interest rate cut will actually occur, and as of the time of writing, the market believes that there is a 50.4% probability that there will not be an interest rate cut next month:

On Friday of last week, the market believed that there was a 66% chance that the Federal Reserve would cut by 25 basis points at its December meeting, so this is a pretty big swing. The increasing doubts about interest rate cuts could also be a contributing factor to Spire’s sell-off this morning.

As we all know, there is an inverse correlation between stock prices and interest rates. Basically, whenever interest rates rise, stock prices typically decline and vice versa. One reason for this is that companies that rely heavily on debt financing for growth end up needing to pay a higher proportion of their cash flows to service their debts when interest rates are high compared to when they are lower. Utility companies such as Spire operate in a highly capital-intensive industry and they need to continually invest in infrastructure upgrades in order to increase their revenues. This is because regulations basically have the company’s rates charged to customers set at a level that allows it to earn a specific return on the value of its assets. This forces utilities to continually invest in infrastructure, which is at least partially financed through the issuance of debt. The higher interest rates are, the more it costs utilities to finance this debt.

Perhaps more importantly, though, is the fact that utility companies are generally considered to be safe companies that exhibit fairly low growth and pay out respectable yields. In the eyes of many investors, this essentially makes them similar to bonds. However, bonds are senior to utility common equities in the capital stack, so they are still considered to be safer securities to hold. Whenever bond yields rise, the dividends of utility companies become less attractive by comparison, and their prices tend to decline. The reverse is true, as low bond yields will drive risk-averse investors out of bonds and into utility stocks in search of yield.

Admittedly, bond yields fell on Friday morning:

However, we can see that short-term yields are up on the week, which reflects the weakening confidence that investors have about the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates in December. The decline in bond yields this morning is likely at least partially due to a flight to safety going on. Spire is clearly not benefiting from this as it was down this morning (and still is as of 11:30 a.m. EST), but it is still possible that the company’s stock has gotten caught up in a flight to safety as investors sell common equities across the board and purchase bonds.

Earnings Report Analysis

As was just established, Spire shares seem to be selling off on the same day that it reported its earnings. While it is possible that this is being partially driven by disappointment with the company’s earnings report, there are some other factors that could be playing a role here, and the sell-off coinciding with earnings might be a coincidence. Let us take a look at the company’s earnings report and see to what degree a sell-off based on earnings alone is justified.

Here are the highlights from Spire’s fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report:

  • Spire reported total operating revenue of $334.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2024. This represents a 13.72% increase over the $293.8 million that the company reported for the prior-year quarter.
  • The company reported an operating loss of $500,000 for the most recent period. This compares very unfavorably to the $19.8 million operating profit that the company made in the year-ago quarter.
  • Spire unveiled a new capital spending program for the 2026 to 2030 period. This capital spending program is expected to increase its rate base from approximately $8.2 billion today to $10.7 billion by the end of the 2030 fiscal year.
  • The company announced its intention to have total capital expenditures of $11.2 billion over the next ten years.
  • Spire reported a net loss available to common shareholders of $43.4 million for the fourth quarter of 2025. This compares quite unfavorably to the $29.6 million that the company reported in the fourth quarter of the 2024 fiscal year.

The first thing that we are likely to notice here is that with the exception of revenues, the company generally reported weaker results than in the year-ago quarter. Spire tends to focus more on a non-GAAP metric called “adjusted earnings,” rather than on the GAAP numbers in its press release and earnings conference calls, so only limited information about the causes of the deterioration in its GAAP operating income and net income was provided in the earnings report. However, the company did make the following statement in its press release:

Due to the seasonal nature of natural gas demand and the timing of regulatory recover in our gas utility business, we typically incur a loss in our fiscal fourth quarter ended September 30.

A look at the company’s historical financial reports does indeed show that Spire very frequently reports a loss in the July to September quarters:

FY 2025

FY 2024

FY 2023

Net Income

$(39.8)

$(25.9)

$(31.1)

(Figures in millions of U.S. dollars and only correspond to the three-month period that ended on September 30 of the respective year.)

Please note that the figures provided above are the company’s net income (net loss in this case) for each of the given periods. This differs from the “net loss available to common shareholders” figure that I provided in the highlights. The biggest difference here is that the “net income available to common shareholders” figure is usually equal to the company’s net income minus the dividends paid to the preferred stockholders (about $3.7 million per quarter). There is also the possibility that participating securities will also have a small amount of income or loss that accrues to them ($100,000 in the most recent quarter), but this is not always the case. In cases where some of the special participating securities have a net income or net loss attributable to them, it will also be taken out of the net income that common shareholders are allocated. For the fourth quarter of 2025, it looks like this:

Q4 2025

Net Income (Loss)

$(39.8)

$3.7

(Loss) Income Allocated to Participating Securities

$(0.1)

Net (Loss) Income Available to Common Shareholders

$(43.4)

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars.)

Spire is not the only natural gas utility that typically reports rather weak results for the July to September quarter. In fact, most pure-play natural gas utilities report weak results during that quarter. There is a very big reason for this, and it comes from the fact that these are among the hottest months of the year. One of the biggest uses of natural gas is in space heating. Many consumers and businesses use natural gas furnaces or boilers to heat internal spaces. During the July to September period, there is not a lot of need for interior heating in most areas of the country. Indeed, in most of the country, it is hot enough that most interior spaces need to be cooled rather than heated. As such, natural gas utility companies tend to sell much less natural gas during these three months than during any other time of the year. A natural gas utility company’s revenues come from billing its customers an amount that is based on the actual amount of natural gas that they consume. As consumption of natural gas is lowest during the July to September period, natural gas utilities have lower revenues during these three months than is the case during most other three-month periods. It is entirely possible that, due to these seasonally lower revenues, the utility company will not have enough revenue coming in to fully cover its expenses. This appears to be what happened with Spire during the most recent quarter.

Growth Thesis Update

As mentioned in the highlights, Spire released an updated investment program that it intends to use to spur its growth. In accordance with its new growth program, Spire intends to spend $4.8 billion over the 2026 to 2030 period:

As shown, this is expected to grow the company’s rate base from $8.2 billion today to $10.7 billion by 2030. This is only a $2.5 billion increase in the company’s rate base, but it will require it to spend approximately $4.8 billion. There are a few reasons for this, but the most important of them is that capital assets and utility infrastructure depreciate over time. For the fourth quarter alone, Spire reported that it had total depreciation and amortization expenses of $76.5 million. In order to keep its rate base stable, the company must invest at least an amount equivalent to its depreciation and amortization. In order to grow the rate base, it has to both cover D&A expense and spend extra for growth. This is one of the major reasons why rate base growth will always be lower than the company’s capital expenditures.

In addition to this, we should also consider that the company may be retiring assets over the period. Whenever an asset is retired, it is removed from the rate base, and the company has to spend even more money to replace it. Rate base growth will be lower than capital expenditures for this reason as well.

Alongside its earnings report, Spire also released a projection of its capital spending plan over the next ten years. Naturally, this one is significantly larger, as the company plans to spend a massive $11.223 billion over the 2026 through 2035 period:

We can clearly see that the bulk of this spending is expected to come after 2030, as fully $6.45 billion is scheduled to take place over the 2031 to 2035 period. This is also quite normal for a utility company, as utilities generally have to increase their spending over time as their rate base grows. After all, the larger the rate base, the more depreciation and amortization that the company will have, and thus the more that they have to spend in order to offset the depreciation and keep their rate base stable.

Spire’s management believes that the new growth plan that it has laid out will allow it to grow its earnings per share at a 5% to 7% average rate over the next decade. If the company is successful in this, then it is likely that its dividend will grow at a similar rate over the period. This is higher than the current rate of inflation, which is quite nice for anyone depending on the company to provide them with an income. After all, we need the company’s dividend to grow at least at the same rate as inflation in order to maintain its purchasing power. While I do expect inflation to tick up further above today’s 3% year-over-year rate, I somewhat doubt that it will be in the 5% to 7% range for the next decade. Thus, Spire should be able to do a reasonably good job of providing retirees and others in need of income with a growing source of income that meets their needs.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, Spire will grow its earnings per share at a 7.77% rate over the next three to five years. This is a bit higher than Spire itself projects, but it is not altogether unreasonable. If the company actually achieves this rate of growth, then it has a price-to-earnings growth ratio of 2.25. Here is how that compares to some of the company’s peers:

Company Name

PEG Ratio

Spire

This comparison suggests that Spire is not only reasonably priced relative to its peers, but it is actually a bit cheap, as anyone buying the company’s shares at the current price will end up paying less for the company’s forward growth than anyone who buys one of the other peer companies.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant system also appears to suggest that Spire is relatively cheap today. The company has a B- grade using this system:

This actually puts the company at a buy level:

Here is how it compares to its peers under this valuation system:

Perhaps interestingly, we can see that all of these companies, except for Atmos Energy, appear to be quite reasonably priced. Spire is far from being the most attractively valued in this group, though, as companies such as New Jersey Resources and Northwest Natural Holding appear to be more attractively priced. However, both of these companies have lower growth rates than Spire, a factor that may diminish their appeal to many investors. In particular, the lack of growth might make it more challenging for them to grow their dividends more rapidly than inflation.

Overall, then, Spire does not appear to be particularly expensive at the current level.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the market’s initial reaction to Spire’s fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report was rather negative as the company’s stock sold off dramatically during the pre-market trading session on the same day. However, it is possible that there were other factors at play here, including concerns about the viability of generative artificial intelligence and the very high expenses of constructing data centers and powering the computing equipment in these data centers. Spire’s stock price has been run up a lot over the past twelve months, which is likely to have been at least partly driven by the potential that it could become a supplier of natural gas for on-site power generation for Kansas City data centers.

If the artificial intelligence narrative fails to live up to the current hype, then some of the growth that the market is hoping for might not actually occur. This could explain some of the early trading action. Fortunately, Spire still appears to be on track to deliver 5% to 7% earnings per share growth annually going forward.

data.title: Paysafe: Reported Revenue Returns To Growth In Q3 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Paysafe Limited (PSFE) slumped a stunning 27.56% on November 13, 2025, with weakness in technology stocks and a negative reaction to the company's Q3 2025 earnings creating the perfect storm. When one considers the company's debt-heavy capital structure, however, the move in the company's valuation is not as dramatic.

Case in point, according to Seeking Alpha data, PSFE ended 2024 with an enterprise value of about $3.27 billion, with recent share price weakness and debt reductions taking the enterprise value to about $2.72 billion, or a drop of 16.8% year-to-date (my calculations show a marginally higher enterprise value of $2.85 billion, indicating a slightly smaller enterprise value drop of 12.8%):

For comparison, PSFE's largest peer PayPal Holdings (PYPL) has seen its enterprise value decline by roughly 28% so far in 2025, indicating that on a company-wide basis, Paysafe is still faring better, which is not the conclusion you would get if you look at share prices alone, with Paysafe stock slumping roughly 57% year-to-date, well above the ~23% decline for PayPal:

Paysafe's Q3 2025 results confirm a return to topline growth in USD terms, as elaborated on in my previous coverage from August 2025. While adjusted EBITDA margins and free cash flow conversion rates are near-term negatives, I confirm my Buy rating on Paysafe, driven by:

  • An attractive 9.3% free cash flow yield to enterprise value, increasing to approximately 21.8% when looking at free cash flow to equity.
  • The prospect of a normalization in free cash flow conversion rates to about 65-70%.
  • Fed rate cuts in 2026 increasing the portion of free cash flow reaching common shareholders in 2026-2027.

In this article, I will expand on these points, provide an update how Paysafe's valuation stands relative to PayPal, and discuss risks to the investment case.

Company Overview

You can access all PSFE results on its website here. Paysafe provides a variety of payment services with a focus on the entertainment sector (primarily gaming and gambling). The company reports results in two key segments - Merchant Solutions at 53% of Q3 2025 revenues and Digital Wallets at 47%.

While the two segments are roughly comparable from a revenue standpoint, profitability in Digital Wallets is much higher, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 45%, versus just 21% in Merchant Solutions.

I calculate the company's enterprise value at roughly $2.85 billion, of which 21% is funded with common equity, leaving 79% for net debt.

Q3 2025 Results Overview

Paysafe reported revenue growth in USD terms of 2% Y/Y in Q3 2025. Organic revenue growth, which adjusts for currency effects and interest income, stood at 6% Y/Y. As expected, sales growth translated into better margins, with the adjusted EBITDA margin improving to 29.2%, up 1.6% Y/Y. The only notable disappointment was lower unlevered free cash flow, which slipped 7% Y/Y to $83.6 million in the quarter.

The silver lining with regards to the free cash flow decline is that it was partially driven by higher capex (which is good for future growth), but other than that, cash flow from operations was indeed quite weak at $69.2 million, down 15.5% Y/Y. CFO John Crawford cited timing of receivables and tax refunds in the prior-year quarter as contributing factors.

Setting the weaker free cash flow aside, I believe Q3 2025 results were quite robust. Organic revenue growth, at 6% Y/Y, was stronger than the 5% increase in Q2. Coupled with higher capex, I believe this bodes well for the company's trajectory in Q4 2025 and going into 2026.

2025 Guidance Reduced

While revenue growth did accelerate in Q3 2025, the jump was apparently somewhat below management expectations, with Paysafe reducing its full-year revenue outlook to $1,700-$1,710 million, down 1% relative to the prior outlook. Still, when we factor in that the company reported revenues of $420 million in Q4 2024, the company's guidance envisages growth in USD terms of about 5% Y/Y in Q4 2025, which again I think is quite encouraging:

Weaker revenues will naturally translate into lower adjusted EBITDA, which is now expected at $425-$430 million, down 9% relative to the prior midpoint. This will result in a weaker adjusted EBITDA margin, as discussed on the conference call:

Adjusted EBITDA increased 7% to $126.6 million. Our third quarter results benefited from a licensing deal, which contributed approximately $10 million to revenue and adjusted EBITDA, offsetting the headwind from ongoing business mix and the divestiture, which has less impact in the second half. As a result, adjusted EBITDA margin was 29.2%, up 160 basis points year-over-year. Given what we're continuing to see at the gross margin level for both segments, we don't expect EBITDA margins to be at this level in Q4. We expect adjusted EBITDA margins closer to 23% in Q4 and about 25% for the full year.

Overall, I think it is encouraging to see topline and organic revenue growth converging in Q4 2025, although margin weakness is a clear disappointment with regards to near-term financial performance. Still, with a strong revenue trajectory going into year-end 2025, I believe margins should begin to recover in 2026, as confirmed on the conference call:

Overall, this is not where we plan to be from a margin perspective, nor does it reflect the potential of our business model and the operating leverage that we can deliver.

Paysafe Valuation

As discussed in the Introduction section, employing an enterprise-level valuation approach is, in my opinion, the best course of action, justifying the company's very high leverage.

Over the past 12 months, unlevered free cash flow stands at $265.3 million, reflecting a 62% conversion of Adjusted EBITDA into free cash flow:

I should note the conversion in Q3 2025 stood at 66%, with 65-70% expected for Q4 2025. As such, the $265.3 million is likely a depressed number, but conservatively, I will use it in valuation.

Relative to the $2.85 billion enterprise value, we observe that the free cash flow yield stands at roughly 9.3%, which, for a company growing revenues in line with inflation, is overall attractive in my opinion.

Compared with PayPal, which boasts an adjusted free cash flow of about $6.4 billion over the past 12 months (free cash flow yield to enterprise value of roughly 10%), we observe that Paysafe is now more expensive. What's more, Paysafe's larger peer delivered stronger revenue growth, at 7% Y/Y in Q3 2025.

That said, I still believe that Paysafe's valuation is appealing in itself, especially if we assume a marginally stronger free cash flow conversion in 2026. Hence, I still believe the company is worth a Buy rating. Even so, the investment case is now more blurred as Paysafe is trading marginally above the valuation of PayPal, indicating a normalization in free cash flow conversion is already priced in.

Paysafe is furthermore quite attractive for investors with above-average risk tolerance, as free cash flow to equity stands at roughly $130 million over the past 12 months, or a free cash flow yield of roughly 21.8%. This attractiveness is further confirmed by ongoing share repurchases, with the company increasing its repurchase authorization by $70 million alongside Q3 2025 results.

Risks

79% of Paysafe's enterprise value is funded with net debt, making the company exposed to changes in interest rates as well as creditors' perception of its creditworthiness. The good news for Paysafe is that the Fed funds futures price in a Fed funds rate of about 3% by the end of 2026, indicating interest costs, at 5.1% currently, should decline marginally in 2026 and 2027. This, in turn, will allow a greater proportion of free cash flow to reach common shareholders, increasing the already attractive 21.8% common stock free cash flow yield.

As discussed in the Paysafe Valuation section, some normalization in free cash flow conversion is already priced in. Hence, a persistently low free cash flow conversion relative to adjusted EBITDA, such as the 62% observed over the past 12 months, would lead to further share price declines.

Last but not least, while revenues are still growing at the moment, the sector-wide decline in valuations seems to indicate investors are worried about performance in future years, perhaps driven by the emergence of AI/crypto-based competitors, which will reduce the demand for legacy payment systems.

Conclusion

While Paysafe's year-to-date share price decline is dramatic, I believe it is largely the result of significant leverage and a drop in sector-wide valuations. That said, investors are clearly worried about weakness in free cash flow conversion and weaker margins going into Q4 2025.

Looking ahead to 2026, topline growth in USD will increasingly converge with organic growth, although Fed rate cuts will likely result in incremental weakness in reported revenue growth.

I confirm my Buy rating on Paysafe, with the company offering an attractive 21.8% free cash flow yield to equity, which is set to increase amid Fed rate cuts and business growth.

Key risks in the investment case include high leverage, no normalization in free cash flow conversion, and the emergence of disruptive AI/crypto-based payment competitors.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: FDLO: Leading The Low-Volatility Category, But Lagging The Benchmark

This article updates my review published in April 2025 in light of recent performance and current holdings.

FDLO strategy

Fidelity Low Volatility Factor ETF (FDLO) was launched on 09/12/2016 and tracks the Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Factor Index. FDLO has a portfolio of 129 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.30%, and a total expense ratio of 0.16%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

The mutual fund Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Equity Fund (FULVX) is planned to be merged into FDLO on or about 5/8/2026. FULVX shareholders would receive corresponding shares of FDLO. It will not result in a large increase in FDLO’s assets under management (already over $1.4 billion), as FULVX has less than $40 million in AUM.

The fund aims to generate returns similar to the broader market in the long term with less volatility. The purpose is to increase risk-adjusted performance by reducing risk. As described by Fidelity, the largest 1,000 U.S. stocks based on market cap are screened for low historical volatility of prices and earnings based on three metrics in equal weight:

  • 5-year Standard Deviation of Price Returns,
  • 5-year Beta,
  • 5-year Standard Deviation of EPS.

Constituents are selected and weighted by an iterative process considering the factor score, sectors, and market capitalization. The index is rebalanced semi-annually. This article will use as a benchmark the Russell 1000 Index, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

FDLO is almost exclusively invested in U.S.-domiciled companies (99% of asset value), mostly in large and mega-caps (78%). The sector breakdown of FDLO is very close to the benchmark’s, as plotted on the next chart, pointing to a sector-neutral weighting methodology. The portfolio is overweight in technology with 34.1% of assets, while other sectors are below 14%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, with 39% of asset value in the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table. The three heaviest positions (Apple, Microsoft, and Alphabet) weigh between 5% and 8%.
